constraints.analysis_summary: After round 1 (empty guess), we have no information about the secret. All colors are equally possible, and no constraints have been established. We need to gather data by guessing actual colors.

Combined with round 1 (empty guess, no info), we now know that among red, blue, green, yellow, exactly three are in the secret and one is absent. Exactly one guessed position is correct. Two other present colors are misplaced. The fourth secret slot remains unknown. No specific colors are confirmed or impossible yet.

After round3, we have intersecting constraints from two non-empty rounds. The secret contains exactly three of {red, blue, green, yellow}, and exactly two of {green, yellow, white, black}. This forces at least one of green/yellow to be present. No specific color is confirmed present or absent with certainty. The exact locked positions from both rounds are still unknown, but we know one position is correct from each round. The remaining unknowns include the absent color from round2, the composition of the fourth secret slot, and the exact placements of the locked and misplaced colors.

After round 4, combined constraints force white to be absent and yellow to be present. Two main cases remain: Case A (green present, black absent, one of red/blue present) leading to three distinct colors (yellow, green, and one of red/blue) with one duplicate; Case B (green absent, black present, both red and blue present) leading to four distinct colors (yellow, black, red, blue). The locked positions from rounds 2,3,4 are still unknown but must be consistent with the chosen scenario. No specific color beyond white is yet proven impossible.

After round 5, we have further narrowed the secret. White is impossible, yellow is confirmed. Two main consistent scenarios remain: (A) secret contains yellow, green, red, and a duplicate of one of these; (B) secret contains yellow, red, blue, and black, all distinct. No single locked position is certain across both scenarios. The three present colors from round5 (including yellow and exactly two of {green, black, red}) are all misplaced relative to their guessed positions.

After round 6, the secret is completely solved. The set is {black, yellow, red, blue} in order [yellow, blue, red, black]. All previous feedback is consistent with this assignment. No further ambiguity.
After round 7, the secret is fully confirmed as [yellow, blue, red, black]. All prior feedback is consistent. No more ambiguities remain.
After round 8, the secret is completely confirmed as [yellow, blue, red, black]. All previous feedback is consistent with this assignment. No further ambiguities exist.

Step 3: IDENTIFY MISPLACED COLORS - Feedback misplaced=2 implies the two remaining present colors (among the three present) are in the secret but not at their guessed positions. Their identities depend on which color is locked and which is absent.

We need to determine: the absent color, the locked position, the correct positions for the two misplaced colors, and the identity/position of the fourth color.

constraints.analysis_summary: After round 1 (empty guess), we have no information about the secret. All colors are equally possible, and no constraints have been established. We need to gather data by guessing actual colors.

Combined with round 1 (empty guess, no info), we now know that among red, blue, green, yellow, exactly three are in the secret and one is absent. Exactly one guessed position is correct. Two other present colors are misplaced. The fourth secret slot remains unknown. No specific colors are confirmed or impossible yet.

After round3, we have intersecting constraints from two non-empty rounds. The secret contains exactly three of {red, blue, green, yellow}, and exactly two of {green, yellow, white, black}. This forces at least one of green/yellow to be present. No specific color is confirmed present or absent with certainty. The exact locked positions from both rounds are still unknown, but we know one position is correct from each round. The remaining unknowns include the absent color from round2, the composition of the fourth secret slot, and the exact placements of the locked and misplaced colors.

After round 4, combined constraints force white to be absent and yellow to be present. Two main cases remain: Case A (green present, black absent, one of red/blue present) leading to three distinct colors (yellow, green, and one of red/blue) with one duplicate; Case B (green absent, black present, both red and blue present) leading to four distinct colors (yellow, black, red, blue). The locked positions from rounds 2,3,4 are still unknown but must be consistent with the chosen scenario. No specific color beyond white is yet proven impossible.

After round 5, we have further narrowed the secret. White is impossible, yellow is confirmed. Two main consistent scenarios remain: (A) secret contains yellow, green, red, and a duplicate of one of these; (B) secret contains yellow, red, blue, and black, all distinct. No single locked position is certain across both scenarios. The three present colors from round5 (including yellow and exactly two of {green, black, red}) are all misplaced relative to their guessed positions.

After round 6, the secret is completely solved. The set is {black, yellow, red, blue} in order [yellow, blue, red, black]. All previous feedback is consistent with this assignment. No further ambiguity.
